text: 0.3g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.
3.3g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
3.1g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
minerals are basically not part of the case here.

the upsides first: very low downside whole food because vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff. helps add volume without much calorie cost because that makes vegetables easier to use often. good volume for the calories because that matters for satiety and general usefulness.

on the weaker side: usually needs pairing to make a full meal because most vegetables are not complete by themselves. freshness and prep can matter because practical friction is still a real downside. taste and texture can limit compliance because a strong paper profile still needs real-world repeatability.
C tier. Best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
